CURRENT AFFAIRS,

A Highland major, impressed by the fact that
Unionists and Protestants of all sects in Ireland do not
take kindly to the Irish language, wrote, in the inno-
oence of his heart, a letter to 4lf Foxz. Alf printed the
letter, and devoted a leading article to it. The libeller
of Canon M‘Inerney commenced its leading article by a
question—"“Ts it, desirable that Irish Unionists should
reconsider the attitude of suspicion and dislike which,
as a body, they have adopted to the policy and opera-
tions of the Gaelic League?’ What follows is an ab-
normal medley.  4If Foz is Arnott’s paper, and the
Arnotts, we understand, are Scotch, or of immediate
Scotch extraction, and the editor of Alf is an importa-
tion. The very term “Irish Unionist”’ is a fallacy.
There are, of course, exceptions to prove the rule that

~ there are no Irish Unionists extant—the rule is that
those who go by the misnomer of “Irish Unionists’’
are the English in Ireland; and the attitude of the
_English in Ireland is merely the attitude of a privileged
race quartered on a country towards a race which they

and theirs tried to conquer, and still wish to exterminate
or absorb. Alf remarks that “the majority of Irish
Unionists are neither bigots nor fools.”” We doubt
very much the bigot part of the proposition, and as for
the fool part we are rather inclined to admit that they
are not such fools as the Nationalists who allow them-
solves to be hoodwinked and circumvented by them.
However, it is enough for our purpose to point out to
AIf that the majority of “ Irish Unionists’’ are merely
England’s Faithful Garrison in Ireland, and that is the
simple reason why the Irish language is to them as a
red rag is to a bull.

When play-actors’ duty is to be done in some parts of
the United States, those who take the part of Stage
Irishmen have not a pleasant timo of it. A thing by
the name of “ McFadden’s Flats ’ is apparently particu-
larly obnoxious, though probably it is just the sort of
thing that would be voted “beastly funny’’ by our
Berties and Chawlies at this side. It is well that some
of our amateur as well as professional entertainers do
not perform in New Haven. We read in the press:—
“ Angered because Mayor Studley refused to bar the
performance of ‘ MFadden’s Flats ’ at the New Haven
Theatre to-night, members of the twelve Irish Societies
of the city formed in a solid phalanx and fought for
nearly half an hour with fifty policemen who were on
duty there. Most of the policemen in the squad were
Germans. The protest of the Irish Societies was made
because the play, they say, held the Irish up to ridicule,
and had started riots in other cities.”” It is well for
some people over here that Irishmen of that calibre are
scarce in the green isle. %

The Freeman’s young man wrote some simply de-
plorable bosh about the Furniture Exhibition at the
Spring Show. Lord Aberdeen, attended by the usual
trail of polite flunkeys, opened the Furniture Exhibi-
tion. He was received by a number of those who no
doubt enjoy bowing to anyone dressed out in a little
brief authority, and Lord Bung (Ardilaun) read an
address. Lord Aberdeen in reply made a sweet speech.
If sweet speeches were a fair exchange for furniture then
Irish industry would be in a flourishing condition. His
Excellency laid the butter thick on  the eldest bigot

of them all.”” He said:— S
With regard to the fact that thia exhibition is held under: the
auspices of the Royal Dublin Society, one cannot help remarking
that this furnishes but ono of many tokens of the comprehensive-
ness of your operations; and I am reminded of another inoidental
passing illustration of that celebrity to which I have referred,
namoly, that when a fow weeks ago a very important question
connected with agriculture was being disoussed in Parliament ono
of tho speakers alluded to the opinion on that subject which had
been given out by the Royal Dublin Society as the opinion of an
in:titution known to everybody, and as an op.inion_which would
neoessarily carry weight on tho subject under discussion.

Well, we have our doubts about the comprehen-
siveness’’ of the operations of the Royal Dublin Society
in one respect. Wo would refer Lord Aberdeen to the
Leaper of September 27th, 1902, for some interesting
particulars of ‘this comprehensive’’ Royal Dublin
Bigot Society. In that article, entitled The Eldest
Bigot of them all,”’ it is stated :—“ No, there is not so
much as a deputy office-boy in the place, save of the
dividend-paying faith. There is, I have heard, one
omployé of the Society who belongs to the Idolatrous
sect; he is a caretaker of some part of the premises. I
have not been able to hear of any other.”” Further on
in that article in our issue of September 27th, 1902, we
read :—“ I cannot say if an odd Popish female gets any
employment as charwoman, offico cleaner, or such like;
if she does, I suppose due care is taken fo seo that, she
does not use any Idolatrous dusters, and that she wields

St. Kevin's Park, Kilma.cud, Co. Dublin, is the scene 
of many activities. From this out, until the end of the 
summer, we suppose lady cyclists from town will be 
visiting it in droves, and ha.ving ,their tea there, or 
perhaps staying there half the day. The Holiday House 
Department, if we may call it so, will also, no doubt, 
be filling ·up shortly. Aliy lady who might go for a 
week or so to Kilmacud, and who does not do so, cannot 
possibly be kept away by the prices, for the terms per 
week for board and residence run from 12s. 6d. to 
24s. 6d. 
.As our readers know, attached to this house 
is a school for training domestic servants, or ra.ther for 
training raw material in-to the state of being a domes-tic 
servant. 
This department is connected with the Dub-
lin County Council, which gives grants for the training 
of a. certain number of servaruts. Another department 
of -this now complex institution deals with· the training 
of first-class students .in domestic science,· and we regret 
to see by .the report for 1905, that this branch of work 
• has not been so successful as the Holiday Home and 
. Training of Servants Departments. 
This is certainly a 
pity, though perhaps it is not uncharacteristic of a 
country overcrowded 
with 
" accomplished '' young 
women looking through the screens of their parlour win-

iq.come would suit one of them; but the poor man is 
expected to be quite satisfied with a dainty mass of in-

more of him ,than she does of chops. The fee for the 
full courses of training from September 1 to July 31st 
is £35; shorter courses can also be taken. 
This com-
plex a.nd beneficent institution by the Dublin moun-

of Father Glendon, O.P . 
The present able Chairman 
of the Committee is Father D. R . Magennis, O.P. 
As 
an indication of the Irish atmosphere of the place, it ia 
pleasant to note by the 1906 Report that in accordance 
with the wishes of the founders, the girls in training 
have an opportunity of learning Irish, and a teacher 
attends each week on Thursday evenings for two hours.

Way ** man of the Deplorable might say, by a paragraph
1n a recent issue of the Cathol'icg T{meys,. }iIex}w)e ar% tlljle
first two sentences of the paragraph:—“In the course
of a dialogue on the Resurrection of Christ which he
contributes to the “ Hibbert Journal,”” Mr. T. W. Rel-
lestog takes ‘occasion to censure the Catholic Church,
especially upon two grounds. In the first place, ho de-
f:l&res that it paralyses thought, and in the mext that

Cz_xbhohc}sm only exists at all at the presont day by
tacitly suppressing half its creed.’ ”’ ‘What, by the way,
is the “Hibbert Journal?’ We do not remember to
have heard the name before. Perhaps it may be one of
the latest “long felt wants’’ in the Jjournalistic line!
What a compliment is paid to the religion professed by
us poor “Idolators’’ by the fact of the perpetual nag-
ging that certain people keep up concerning it.  Of
course 1t is of no interest to anyone beyond his imme-
diate circle of relations and personal friends what Mr.
Rolleston thinks of anything—we have no doubt that
he even bores many of his friends. Now that such an
eminent monument of the futility of Trinity College
education has censured the poor old Catholic Church we
trust that he will redouble his efforts in the interests of
the “ Branch of the Five Protestants.’’

_The North East Ulster problem keeps hobbing up not-
withstanding how people may wish to protend that it
does not exist. At the “ National’” Teachers’ Congress
a Mr. Simpson, during the discussion on the question of
the Irish language in the schools, said:— There were
present delegates who had not the privilege of being of
directly Irish descent. Well, he was one of them. He
represented there a section of the North of Ireland that
did not know a word of Irish spoken around him, and he
did not suppose he ever would. Why should his son or
daughter be compelled to learn Irish when it was of no
possible use in the North? In this matter he thought
the North should have a little say, and to make Irish
a compulsory subject was going a little too far, and more
especially as Irish was of no commercial value at all.””
Thus spoke, and spoke frankly, that portion of the
British people who are settled in North-East Ulster.
For our part we do not see much reason in pretending
that we do not see where the map of Ireland is marked
Britain. We may talk about all creeds and all classcs
and the rest of it just as the hopeful Britishers talk of
the United Kingdom, and of the Britons of Connacht!
But it is only talk. We know that the poor Britishers
are talking nonsense; the Mr. Simpsons of the North-
East know that our yearnings for a united Ireland, in-
cluding Belfast, etc., are built of stuff that dreams are
made of.

His Eminence Cardinal Logue was in fine fighting
fettle at Dundalk last week. Evidently he can judge
Mr. Bryce’s sweet mouthings for what they are worth: —

We have had changes of Government and new Ministers, but my
experiencd since I was a boy—and it is a very long experienco
now—that it matters very little to Irishmen, and matters even
still less to the Irish Catholics, what Government is in power in
England. The Tories, when in power, had a strong tendency to
place us under the heel of the minority in this country; and tho
‘Whigs or Liberals, when they get into power, have a very strong
tendency to follow in the footsteps of the Tories. So that whether
1t is the Whigs or the Tories are in offico in England, we find that
we are still under tho blight of Castle rule, and still subjected to.
tho withering breath of ascendancy in this country.

The country will yet conclude that the effective way
for Ireland to fight is to fight the Ascendancy in Ire-
land—fight England’s Faithful Garrison and give up the
namby-pamby and cowardly humbug that claims in a
cloud of fine rhetoric the Orange-Scotch anti-Irishman
as a fellew countryman. He is no more an Irishman
than the Jew financiers of South Africa were Boers.

His Eminence also said:—If we take up the last
forty years for instance, during which period we have
had Liberals and Tories in power alternating with each
other, and if we go over the list of those who were ap-
pointed to offices of profit or honour in this country, we
should find that whether ‘Whigs were in oq'ice or Tories
were in office the number of Irish Catholics who were

‘ srmitted i uny vosition of trust or honour or

emolument were as few as the ripe grapes after a well-
gleaned vintage.”” Quite so. These things that are
largely arranged on the Ascendancy side by the organised
forces of Orangemen and Freemasons can only be fought
by the organisation of those on the side of Tolerance,
Justice and Fair Play. We already have an organisa-
tion, the Catholic Defence Society, and if the majority
of the people are worth their salt they will rally to its
side.

Cardinal Logue’s reflections on Royal Commissions are
particularly breezy and will be regarded as convincing
by most sensible people:—

Some years ago wo had a great Government Commission on this
quostion of University education—and my experience of Commis-
sions is that they are a political institution invented for the pur-
poso of hanging up questions that are ripe for solution—and now
I beliove we are going to havo another Commission, under the
Liberal Government, on some malters bearing on the same sub-
ject. Somo few days ago I was written to, sounded to see if I
had the namo of any Catholic lay gentleman to suggest for this
Commission, not by anyone in authority, I may say, but by a
politician; and I said I had not. I could suggest no name for
these Commissions. I know them too well, and I know that they
aro simply a cheat and a humbug, and I would have nothing
whatever to do with them. Commissions for the purpose of
gathering knowledge on this great burning question, which has
s0 long remained unsettled hero in Ireland, are unnecessary. They
have plenty of knowledge with regard to it. If they had a Com-
mission that could make statesmen honest, that could make states-
men do their duty, that could inspire statesmen with principles of
oven-handed justice, I would go for that Commission.

We commend this incisive and forcible utterance to
the sweet-mouthed Mr. Bryce and the sympathetic Earl
of Aberdeen.  After all, what we want from Chief
Secretaries as well as from Irish patriots is the verb to
do and not the mere verb to say.

Mr. William Moore, K.C., the live bigot and ex-M.P.,
was on the stump at Rasharkin last week. ~Our readers
may be interested in Moore, ex-M.P., on Sloan, ex-
Belfast Custom House steps orator. Moore—William
not George—said : —

Mr. Sloan was called to account. He found he could do nothing:
in Parliament without the party, and he protested that he had
been led into error unintentionally, expressed his regret, and
undertook for tho future to act loyally with the party. From that
time on they had no troublo with him. He came to heel like a
tame poodle ‘whenever he was whistled for. The Ulster party
treated him as-one of themselves, and in return since last July
they knew how in that very constituency he had ocarried out his
protestations of loyalty. There was nothing in Mr, Sloan. Some-
one else was whistling to him now, and the working man would
presently find out that he was merely, as before, docility itself,
obeying orders, though they now came from the opposite camp.

This is interesting reading for low-down “ Idolators’”
who lack the ““economic sense.”” However, the “tame
poodle,”” as Mr. Moore, ex-M.P., styles Mr. Sloan, late
of the Custom House steps, may be left to have it out
with Mr. Moore, ex-M.P. How these anti-“ Idolators **
love one another! 5

It is quite remarkable, particularly at this time of
day, how many people send us particular information,
but fail to give us their names and addresses in confi-
dence. Probably in all cases, or nearly all, the infor-
mation is correct, and is sent in good faith. However,
correspondents cannot reasonably expect us to act on
their information, and take all the possible risks of
doing so when they are so very careful themselves that
even in confidence they refuse to let us know who they
are.

What is the value of a good resolution in Ireland?
A few weeks ago the Donegal Board of Guardians
passed a resolution that, in dealing with applications
for posts in its gift, preference should in future ‘be
given to candidates having a knowledge of Irish.. Well,
almost immediately a vacancy for a doctor occurred in
the Pettigo district, and the advertisement was issued.
The advertisement duly stated that preference would
be given to the candidate having a knowledge of Irish,
and, so far, all was fair and square. = The election came
off on Saturday, April 16th. Three candidates were
proposed, one only of whom, Dr. MacAuliffe, had a
‘knowledge of Irish. Dr. MacAuliffe: was proposed by

A curious sidelight is thrown on the emigration 
problem by a little paragraph that appeared in a daily 
paper last Friday. Among the emigrants who left 
Queenstown · on Thursday last by the Teutonic, were 
a number of boys and girls who were travelling on 
prepaid tickets to San Francisco. 
They cared nothing

policy-holders was the establishing of subsidiary trust

officers and trustees were enabled to exploit the policy-

say thab the New York Life had one such subsidiary
company over a dozen years ago, but to the credit of
John A. M‘Call, our dead president, be it said, one of
the first acts of his administration was the abolishment
of this subsidiary company. Having, therefore, tho
past history of our great American companies in view,
it behoves the founders of any new insurance organisa-
tion in Treland to safeguard policy-holders of future
generations from the dangers through which we here in
the States have just passed.

To come to the kernel of the rcasons why I advocate
a stock company for Treland, under her present condi-
tions rather than a purely mutual company—I should
say that my primary reason arises from the difficulty
which I conceive would be encountered in raising the
initial amount of money necessary to start an organisa-
tion which, iu all human probability, would have the
elements of future success in it. Human nature is not
given to indulging in altruistic caprices, and I judge
you will find it very difficult in Treland to get a suffi-
cient number of capitalists to advance jointly the
amount necessary for starting the enterprise. The his-
tory of all purely mutual companies began in the com-
ing together of a few capitalists and their “putting up,”
as we say here, the necessary amount of capital; but in
all such instances they controlled the officers of the
newly-formed organisation for themselves and their
friends, so that ostensibly whilst they posed as public
benefactors they always looked out for their own in-
terests, in other words, they were not altruistic. The
New York Life and the Mutual Lifo both had their
origin in this way. For my part, 1 should not like to
see a mutual company started in Ireland on this or
similar basis. The stock of this company, if it becomes
a stock company, should be widely diffused, as far as
practical, among small investors all over the country.
Each investor becomes an interested party and a friend
of the newly-formed business, and will naturally later on
influence his acquaintances to insure in the new com-
pany. This advantage of mutual and general help
would be wanting in the case of a mutual company. Of
course, I consider a mutual company the ideal insur-
ance company, and on this account I would suggest
that in the constitution of the new Irish company pro-
vision be made for the retirement of the stock and the
ecmplete mutualisation in the future of the company.

Whether these provisions be absolute or optional with
the policy-holders is another question on which a more

‘thorough investigation of the English companies would

enable me to form a better judgment. It is not wise,
however, to be led astray by the clamour that recent
events have given rise to against stock companies. As
a matter of fact, all our successful insurance organisa-
tions that make a specialty of industrial insurance are
slock companies.  For example, the Prudential, the
Metropolitan, of this city (the largest of them all), the
“Etna,” and the Travellers, both of Hartford, etc. -

In order to make the proposition attractive to in-
vestors, the stock from the very outset should have a
guarantee of five per cent. This percentage and more,
and in addition a' large dividend to policy-holders, can
bo carned if the business is economically and conser-
vatively conducted, and there is no reason why the stock
should not enhance in value as time progresses. The
general public are not at all aware what a large margin
there is in the industrial department of life insurance.
All our large industrial companies had, comparatively
speaking, small beginnings, and are to-day a marvel of
finance. True, the conditions are not the same here as
in Ireland.  Our large centres of population have
afforded the opportunity which all the great centres of
population afford in the exploiting of the business
amongst the masses, and in the accumulation of im-
mense reserves made up, in large part, of the pennies
of the thrifty poor in ever-increasing proportions, and
extending to them in return benefactions in the form
of protection to the family in the event of the death of
the wage-earner, till the world stands astonished to-day
at the wonderful achievements of our insurance com-
panies.  Though the conditions are not of the same
character in Ireland as here, still the main lines of
effort in the piling up of business are practically the
sameo the world over, and what has been accomplished
here can be accomplished on a smaller scale in Ireland
if a similar motive power be adopted. The entire ques-
tion of the founding of an Irish insurance organisation
should appeal to every true lover of his country. We
are all justly indignant at the stream of emigration
that leaves Ireland year by year, and threatens, if not
impeded, the utter annihilation of the race in its cradle-
land; but what wonder that you should have emigra-
tion when the English and Scotch insurance companies
are preying on the very vitals of your motherland, and
withdrawing the life-blood of trade and commerce in
the shape of millions of dollars annually paid in pre-
miums and invested in England.

When viewed from this standpoint, the establishment
of a national insurance organisation becomes a national
duty. M. A. O’BYRNE.

THE DESERTED ABBEY.

HE programme of the Abbey Theatre tells you
that it seats five hundred and sixty-two persons.
On Wednesday evening it seated sixty-two, and Wed-
nesday was the second night of a new play. Now there
is clearly something wrong about this. Sixty-two per-
sons do not make a nation, and unless the Abbey
Theatre can attract a larger auditory than its prede-
cessor, the Coroner’s Court, the movement for an Irish
national theatre will remain chiefly a journalistic one.

‘What ¢s the matter with the Abbey Theatre?

First let us take the plays themselves .. To me their
attractions seems somewhat limited. There is fine writ-
ing in Baile’s Strand, and the plot is a splendid subject
for drama, the slaying of son by father. But the
treatment seemed weak, and without real tragic
strength. G. B. Shaw would, moreover, say that the in-
troduction of the blindman and the fool brought the play
down to the level of Shakespeare. This is my personal
view. But one that was with me, whose opinion I value
highly, maintained on the contrary that Mr. Yeats had
admirably caught the romantic spirit, and that it was
one of the best plays the theatre has yet produced. We
should ever recognise our own limitations, or some of
them, and perhaps I resemble more the five hundred
than the sixty-two in that I am unable to appreciate
the romantic spirit. I cannot, for instance, as I have
mentioned before, admire Becket. But in any view I
am sure the play was much superior to Oliver Twist.

The second play was a novel experiment—namely, a
translation by Lady Gregory of Maliére’s Médecin
malgré lui into the Anglo-Irish dialect. Translations
of Moliére are seldom very successful, for it is all but
impossible to reproduce the subtle keenness of his dia-
logue, and I do not think the present experiment justi-
fied itself. How far the “low comedy,”’ or broad farce
of the play as presented, was due to Moliere how far to
Lady Gregory, and how far to the actors, I cannot say,
as I do not happen to have read the original. But if it
be due to Moliere, it is the first failure of his I have
come across. Unless Lady Gregory desired to show the
superiority of her own plays—and Hyacinth Halvey is
a hundred times a better play—there was no special rea-
son for producing in the Abbey Theatre a performance
fit only for a pantomime or a music-hall “sketch.’”

The other item on the programme was Mr.
Darley’s playing, and admirably well he plays.

airs’’  repertoire—the orchestral joy of the

HERE was a large attendance at the meeting of
Convocation held in the Royal University, on
Friday, 20th inst., and there were some lively scenes.
A few zealots from the North seemed disposed to ob-
ject to the co-option of the two distinguished ecclesias-
tics, Rev. T. A. Finlay and Dr. Hogan, but the good

It has been said that ¢ every country is governed as it
deserves to be governed,” and although we cannot
accept this as a general proposition, there is a certain
amount of truth in it both with reference to Nations
and to Institutions.  Catholics complain, and with
reason, that in Ireland they are not treated as fairly as
Protestants, Presbyterians, Methodists, Jews, Agnostics
and Non-believers. The “ Catholic Defence Society '
has been forced into existence by the necessity for mak-
ing some effort to sccure equality of treatment for Catho-
lics. But on the other hand, we must ask ourselves, do
all Catholics sufficiently exert themselves to secure such
equality? Take this very case of the Royal University.
Some public writers hold that the Royal provides so
poorly for the wants of its students, that it makes little
difference how it is governed, and consequently that at an
election, such as that which took place on the 20th, it
makes very little difference whether a Catholic is, or
is not, elected. We cannot subscribe to this view. Im-
perfect as it is, the Royal is the only University really
“open’’ to Catholics. Protestants loudly assert that
Trinity is now “open to all.”” We do not lay much
weight on such assertions. As a matter of fact Trinity
was always “ open to all,”” but from 1603 to 1793 it was
necessary for a Catholic to formally apostatise before
he could be enrolled. Scores of unfortunate Catholics
did apostatise, and some of their descendants are now
most bitter enemies of Catholicity ; but plain, formal
apostasy is a bigger price than most people liked to
pay for admission to Trinity even when there was no
other University. Accordingly—not through any love
for Catholics or for “equality "’—Trinity in 1793, and
again in 1873, (Fawcett’s Act) reluctantly consented to
changes which permitted Catholics to enroll themselves,
as Catholics, and even to obtain Fellowships, etc. But
these changes in the constitution of Trinity left it
practically as Protestant as ever. The Queen’s Colleges,
too, were “open ’’ to Catholics, just as a public-house
—from the mere fact that it is “ public,”” is “ open *’ to
teetotallers ;. but laws which would tend, directly or in-
directly, to force teetotallers into public-houses would
in themselves be unreasonable and objectionable.

In Ireland, therefore, the Royal University is the
only University which can be used without scruple by
Catholics, and incomplete and imperfect as it is, for
conscientious Catholics there is ne alternative. We
are, therefore, of opinion that while the Royal exists,
Catholics should endeavour to improve it as much as
possible, and, consequently, we think that Catholics
should exert themselves to place as many co-religionists
on the governing body—the Senate—as possible. The
Catholic graduates probably number between 2,000
and 3,000, and they are yearly increasing at a rapid
rate. Of these less than 200 have taken the necessary
steps to place their names on the register of Members
of Convocation, and only 106 took the trouble of
voting for the Catholic candidate Dr. Magennis. It is.
no wondev, therefore, that the government of the Royal
1s falling into the hands of non-Catholics, who now
have a substantial majority on the Senate, and that
Catholics, instead of being in a position to boldly claim.
their rights, must be content to accept them by the
good favour of this dominant party. Is it too much to
hope that Catholic graduates will rouse themselves from
their lethargy? For one pound sterling a graduate can
be entolled for life as a Member of Convocation. This

A NEW IRISH LEADER.

"I‘H'E'notabls utterance of the head of the Irish
Hierarchy recently at Dundalk is, to my mind,
the most thoroughly refreshing and salutary thing we
lmve. been treated to in Ireland this long time.  Irish
public utterances aro too often tainted with a suspicion
of insincerity—insincere hatred of the “ hated ’’ Saxon,
aor insincere good-will towards some momentarily domi-
nant public man—or public mood. Thero is a glorious
absence of anything of that sort from Cardinal Logue’s
Laster Tuesday speech. No man ever spoke out more
straightly, and, within the legitimate bounds of tem-
perance more strongly. -Indeed, I have seldom read a
speech with the whole of which I more thoroughly
agreed. My reason for writing about the speech is
that it offers a stand-point from which we can take a
lcok round upon Irish affairs in general.

Cardinal Logue does not share “the impression that
scems to be general in the country at the present day,
that there are brighter and better times in store for
Ireland.”” Neither do I; neither I think do any of us
who have minds of the sort that insist upon thinking
and observing, and which cannot lazily content them-
selves with vaguely hoping. Hope may be a good tonic,
but it is a thin diet. What are the Liberals going to
do for us? Cardinal Logue seems persuaded that they
will do nothing—save what they can’t help. His Emi-
nence remarked that whether Whigs or Tories were in
office “the number of Irish Catholics who were per-
mitted to rise to any position of trust or honour or
emolument was as few as the ripe grapes after a well-
gleaned vintage.” True; and the excuse of the
Liberals is the excuse of the Tories: the lack of “pro-
perly-qualified ” Catholics!  Liberal, like Tory, takes
care to withhold the University foundation which
should supply the “ proper qualifications.”” And so the
old tale of exclusion goes on from year to year, from
Ministry to Ministry, with the same monotonous ini-
quity. ~Admittedly; of course, the present Government
has its hands full for this year; the Trades Disputes
Bill, and the Education Bill, together with the Budget
and the Estimates and the Procedure question, will
take from them this whole session. But there should
be a definite promise obtained from them that there
shall be an Irish Government Bill nest session.  The
Prime Minister has said something about temperance
legislation, I think for next session, but it should _bc
our Party’s business to see that no first-rank question
comes before the Irish question next year; our Bill
should be the FirsT thing brought in by Ministers noxt
session. I would give the present Government what
“ Chanel >’ has so neatly called “the executor’s year
to put the affairs of the late (un)lamented in order ;
after that—the settlement. If no settlement is offered,
or none that we can with dignity take as even an instal-
ment, then there should be a new policy adopted; not

* simply towards this or that Ministry, but towards Zing-

land.” What that policy should be is not a thing for the

present moment ; the first thing needful is to get a un-

‘animous agreement that there shall be a new policy—a
““ New Departure.” We have been goosed long
enough, about as long as we can stand, and if a scttle-

said, the determination of the expedient hardly belongs
to the present moment; suffice 1t just to say that it
should not be another splutter of boyish rebellion—or
bungled rebellion of any pattern. Nejther do we want
another splurt of dynamitism, nor of any sort of pot-
house plotting. The way does not belong to the moment
to decide; the way, after all, is to strengthen our own
will, for where there is a will a way is always found.
But upon this I insist; the settlement should be called
for noxt year. Delays are proverbially dangerous, and
when a Ministry once gets stale in office, when it
has got “groggy,” and the bye-elections begin to go
against it, any large contentious measure it introduces
is simply a sort of riding for a fall. IF under such cir-
cumstances the Liberals ‘spiritedly”’ went to the
country-—five years off—and the country went against
them, as it probably would when they had kept back
their chief measure till their last year—then we should
have the other “old gang’’ back again, probably with
Jim Campbell for Lord Chancellor of Ireland! If ever
that other “ old gang ” comes back into power, let us in
Ireland take care to have secured some good stout legis-
lative stick to whack them into civility with,

R:turning to the Cardinal’s address, I must say that
I fully share his pessimism about the University Ques-
tion, and also his views about what, in this connection
certainly, T must call the insulting futility of Commis-
sions of Inquiry—as if every man with intellect did not
know the matter to the bottom already! As-the Car-
dinal epigrammatically put it, Commissions “ are a poli-
tical institution invented for the purpose of hanging up
questions that are ripe for solution.”” It seems that
some obliging “ go-between ’’ politician sounded the
Cardinal as to whether he would suggest the name of a
Catholic lay gentleman for another of these goose-traps,
but his Eminence was not in the mood for suggestion.
“T could suggest no name for these Commissions. I
know them too well, and I know that they are simply
a cheat and a humbug, and I would have nothing what-
ever to do with them.”” Neither, I hope, will any other
Bishop.  With one conclusion of Cardinal Logue I
think all of us must agree: that a settlement of the
University question “must be squeezed out of the poli-
tical necessity of some party in England.”” Certainly ;
the mere “ justice of our case '’ and all that sort of thing
will never get it. The Cardinal gave a deserved thrust
to the Irish Party for their treatment of this great
question both here and in Parliament; “mere platonic
resolutions stuck on at the end of their meetings’’ are
the only contributions towards settling the matter they
have made, says the Primate. This trick of fulfilling
our obligations to a cause by passing resolutions in its
behalf has grown upon us all, T fear, in Ireland, but it
is one that must be given up forthwith, if all sincerity
is not to be rotted away out of us. Certainly the Car-
dinal is both earnest and urgent, and urgent on the
University question. “I do not think there will ever
be prosperity, either in industries, or in commerce, or
in any other things that contribute to the material pros-
perity of the country, without a good, sound, practical
bigher education for the young people capable of re-
ceiving it.”” If Ivishmen could only realise the truth
and importance of these words of the Primate, they
would be much hotter on the University Question than
they now are. s

I feel that I have said enough to emphasise the
general importance of the Cardinal’s notable speech.
His Eminence’s scepticism about the “ good times com-
ing ’’ is tonic; his criticism of the Irish Party is tonic;
his earnestness on the University Question is stimulat-
mg, and his criticisms of the Government and their
“ Commissions *’ are refreshing, indeed. We feel that
the Cardinal has only to lead, and we will follow.

ESPECTING your suggestion that Irish-Ameri-
cans could aid Ireland by making a market here
for Trish goods notwithstanding the tariff, I have thisto
say :—In the middle ’eighties we developed a very pro-
mising market for Irish woollens. Some spirited Irish-
American in Boston—among them the late Boylo
O’Reilly—prevailed upon one of the largest wholesale
and retail clothing merchants of the city to begin im-
porting Trish cloth direct from the manufacturers. In
a few years the trado grew to considerable proportions ;
but there was some difficulty experienced by the im-
porter here in getting the samples of the various mills
beforo him from which to make his choice of patterns
for this market. To obviate ‘that difficulty Michael
Davitt, exercising the plain, practical commonsense
with which he is endowed, organised “The Trish Woollen
Manufacturing and Export Company ”’ in 1887 in the
City of Dublin. Davitt invested his own money in this
company, and induced his friends abroad to do likewise.
The company was intended to serve as the general sell-
ing agent of all the Irish woollen factories. The repre-
sentative of the company was welcomed in the leading
American cities, and did a fine business. Some of the
Irish mills, however, refused to sell through the com-
pany, and sent their own special selling agents to Ame-
rica to take advantage of the taste developed for Irish
friezes and tweeds. The products of Irish woollen mills,
or imitations of them, became the fashion for some
years. Then suddenly ““ The Irish Woollen Manufac-
turing and Export Company ’’ burst up; Davitt and
his friends who financed it could whistle for their
money ; the Irish woollen mills ceased sending selling
agents among us, and the gates of the market to Ame-
ricans were practically closed, not by the tariff, but by
the bad business management of certain persons in
Dublin, and the narrow selfishness of Irish woollen
manufacturers themselves. Scarcely any Irish woollens
are sold here now, and most of those are sold under the
designation of Scotch goods.

Now for a short story which may have some interest
for Irish-Irelanders, Sinn Fein Missioners, Irish Indus-
trial Development Associations, and other people of
common sense. Three years ago a brainy, level-headed
shoe manufacturer of this State started to Ireland to
see if he could get Irishmen there to organise a shoe-
manufacturing company, which he was ready to direct
upon American lines, and in which he was willing to
invest one-half or-a large minority of the capital. He
went at the instigation of ono in Boston who wished to
see something practical done towards the establishment
of an Irish industry which could compete without favour
in the British and Irish markets. He went with letters
of introduction from some of the best-known Irish-
Americans, including one from the late Patrick A.
Collins, then Mayor of Boston. He believed that if he
could transport his factory here, including operatives
and machinery, into Ireland, notwithstanding the high
rate of American wages, he could compete with British
or American shoes of the high grades which he manu-
factured here, a portion of which he was exporting to
England and Ireland. But he dlfl not propose to
‘bring to Ireland, provided an Irish company was
formed, more than six of his all-round proficient work-
men, who should be paid American wages from the day
they sailed from Boston. With these six men hun-
dreds of young Irish men and women could be in-
structed in the technique of shoe-making so efficiently
that at the end of twelve months they would stand
nearly first-class in the specific sub-division of shoe-
making, to which they had given their attention. He
calculated by the light of his experience that an Irish

Wo stumbled on some firms, the existence of which

of wood workers showed some specimens of plain, ser-
viceable furniture as well as highly-elaborated work.
Irish-made iron bedsteads were exhibited by Mess
Siddall and Hilton, Ltd., of Dublin. A splendid pair
of iron gates, made to the order of Boss Croker, were
amongst the things exhibited by yet another firm, whose
modesty is such that we heard of them for the first
time last week; the firm is that of Messrs. J. and C.
M‘Gloughlin, Ltd., of Great Brunswick Street. They
are large makers of brass church furniture; they have
sent work to Trinidad and Capetown, and to England,
and yet though our offices were for a considerable time
in Great Brunswick Street, we had never heard of this
firm, which, we are now glad to be told, keeps about 120
men employed. Our manufacturers are too modest and
retiring altogether.

Messrs. Aylesbury, of Edenderry, had a big display of
popular, useful furniture, and they showed the skeleton
of an arm-chair, which is their own patent. Messrs.
Aylesbury, notwithstanding the disastrous fire which
broke out some time back, faced their difficulties with
great pluck. They now employ about 120 hands, and
look forward to an expanding business. School furni-
ture was shown by the firm of T. R. Scott. Amongst
the other interesting exhibits were those of Dun Emer,
Dundrum, and the Youghal Art Metal Works.

The chief impression that the exhibition left on our
mind is that Ireland is making no adequate effort to
solve the problem of Irish furniture for the Irish people.
There ought to be thirty or forty big popular furniture
manufacturers in Ireland instead of the few we have.
Furniture is a branch of industry which we should say
must be done on a rather big scale if it is to meet com-
petition.  Highly-wrought and highly-priced work is
all very well in its limited way, and is to be encouraged,
but it is the man who sets up a home on 30s. or £2 a
week who is the big and abiding market.
